refined_fr.refined_fr: The NGHE solution shall achieve and maintain operational availability of ≥0.97 (97%) over the agreed evaluation period, measured as the ratio of uptime to total operational time, accounting for planned maintenance, unplanned downtime, and safety-driven shutdowns.
refined_fr.rationale: CR-NGHE-004 is a key performance indicator (KPI) that directly impacts customer satisfaction and mission success. Refining it requires explicit definition of the measurement period, inclusion criteria (planned vs. unplanned downtime), and acknowledgment that availability is a system-level emergent property dependent on vehicle reliability, teleoperation robustness, and safety service responsiveness. High safety criticality demands that safety-driven shutdowns are counted as availability loss only if they exceed acceptable thresholds.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Continuous telemetry logging of operational state transitions (ready, operating, maintenance, safety-hold, fault) across all modules; statistical analysis of uptime/downtime over rolling evaluation windows; correlation with maintenance schedules and safety event logs.
sub_frs: FR-NGHE-004.1: The Vehicle subsystem shall maintain mechanical and electrical readiness with mean time between failures (MTBF) ≥ X hours.
FR-NGHE-004.2: The Teleoperation subsystem shall maintain network link availability ≥ 0.99 and command-response latency ≤ Y ms.
FR-NGHE-004.3: The Safety Services subsystem shall detect and respond to hazardous conditions within Z seconds without introducing spurious shutdowns.
FR-NGHE-004.4: The NGHE Solution shall provide real-time availability monitoring and predictive maintenance alerts to enable proactive intervention.

candidate_dps: DP-A: Implement redundant subsystems (dual Vehicle units, backup Teleoperation links, failover Safety Services) with automatic switchover logic.
DP-B: Implement predictive maintenance and condition-based monitoring across all subsystems with scheduled preventive maintenance intervals optimized via reliability analytics.
DP-C: Implement a hierarchical availability management architecture with real-time state monitoring, fault detection, isolation, and recovery (FDIR) at subsystem and system levels.
DP-D: Implement a combination of DP-B and DP-C: condition-based monitoring feeding into a centralized availability orchestrator that coordinates maintenance windows and fault recovery.
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP-D: Implement a combination of condition-based monitoring (DP-B) and hierarchical availability management (DP-C). This approach balances cost, complexity, and effectiveness by using predictive telemetry to inform maintenance scheduling while maintaining a centralized orchestrator that coordinates fault detection, isolation, and recovery across Vehicle, Teleoperation, and Safety Services subsystems.

potential_sources: Telemetry interface must carry health/condition data from all subsystems to the availability orchestrator; tight coupling risk if telemetry schema is not extensible.
Availability orchestrator must coordinate with Safety Services to distinguish safety-driven shutdowns from availability-impacting faults; requires clear interface contract on SafetyAlert semantics.
Maintenance scheduling logic must respect operational priorities and customer constraints; risk of over-optimization if not validated against real-world worksite policies.
coupling_check.notes: DP-D is moderately coupled across subsystems via Telemetry and SafetyAlert interfaces. Coupling is acceptable because it is intentional (system-level orchestration) and can be managed via well-defined interface contracts. No evidence of circular dependencies or hidden feedback loops.

assumptions_risks: Assumption: Telemetry infrastructure can reliably transmit health data from all subsystems with sufficient frequency and fidelity to enable predictive maintenance.
Risk: If telemetry is lossy or delayed, predictive maintenance may fail to prevent failures, degrading availability.
Assumption: Safety Services can distinguish between safety-critical shutdowns (which should not count against availability) and fault-driven shutdowns (which should).
Risk: Ambiguous SafetyAlert semantics could lead to incorrect availability accounting.
Assumption: Maintenance windows can be scheduled without excessive disruption to customer operations.
Risk: If maintenance is too frequent or unpredictable, perceived availability may suffer even if measured availability meets target.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: Refine DP-D to explicitly specify: (1) Telemetry data schema and transmission SLA; (2) SafetyAlert classification scheme (safety-critical vs. fault-driven); (3) Maintenance scheduling algorithm and constraints; (4) Availability calculation formula and measurement period. These details should be captured in sub-level DPs for each sub-FR.

im_boundary.name: NGHE Solution
im_boundary.scope: System-level availability orchestrator and monitoring hub. Owns the real-time state machine, fault detection logic, maintenance scheduling coordination, and availability KPI calculation. Integrates telemetry from Vehicle, Teleoperation, and Safety Services; issues directives to coordinate maintenance and recovery actions.

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Receives health/condition data from Vehicle, Teleoperation, and Safety Services subsystems. Includes operational state, fault codes, performance metrics, and predictive health indicators.

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: Receives safety-critical alerts from Safety Services. Used to classify shutdowns and adjust availability accounting.

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Issues maintenance scheduling directives and fault recovery commands to Vehicle, Teleoperation, and Safety Services subsystems.

external_interfaces.name: Network Link
external_interfaces.details: Communicates with remote monitoring/analytics backend for long-term availability trend analysis and predictive maintenance model updates.

module_containment_scores.notes: Highest containment. NGHE Solution is the natural system-level owner of availability orchestration, monitoring, and KPI calculation. All required interfaces (Telemetry, SafetyAlert, Tasking, Network Link) are already defined at system level. Minimal diffusion risk.

Low containment. Vehicle is a subsystem focused on mechanical/electrical readiness. Assigning system-level availability orchestration to Vehicle would scatter logic across subsystems and create tight coupling. Not recommended.

Low containment. Teleoperation is a subsystem focused on remote command/control. Assigning system-level availability orchestration to Teleoperation would create asymmetric ownership and obscure Vehicle and Safety Services health. Not recommended.

Low containment. Safety Services is a subsystem focused on hazard detection and response. Assigning system-level availability orchestration to Safety Services would conflate safety logic with availability logic, violating separation of concerns. Not recommended.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: The NGHE Solution module is the appropriate owner of DP-D (availability orchestration and monitoring) because: (1) Availability is a system-level emergent property that depends on all subsystems; (2) The orchestrator must have a holistic view of Vehicle, Teleoperation, and Safety Services states; (3) All required interfaces (Telemetry, SafetyAlert, Tasking, Network Link) are already defined at the system level; (4) Centralizing availability logic in NGHE Solution avoids diffusion and maintains clear ownership boundaries.
containment_risks: Risk: If NGHE Solution becomes a monolithic orchestrator, it may become a bottleneck or single point of failure. Mitigation: Design the orchestrator as a distributed state machine with redundant instances if criticality warrants.

Risk: Telemetry interface may become overloaded if all subsystems stream high-frequency health data. Mitigation: Define a tiered telemetry schema (critical, normal, diagnostic) and adaptive sampling rates.

Risk: Maintenance scheduling logic may conflict with customer operational priorities if not properly constrained. Mitigation: Expose scheduling constraints as configuration parameters and validate against worksite policies.
assigned_im.recommended_revision: Specify the internal architecture of NGHE Solution's availability orchestrator: (1) Define the state machine (states: Ready, Operating, Maintenance, SafetyHold, Fault, Recovery); (2) Define the fault detection algorithm (threshold-based, anomaly-based, or hybrid); (3) Define the maintenance scheduling algorithm (greedy, constraint-based, or ML-based); (4) Define the availability calculation formula and measurement window. These should be captured in sub-level IMs (e.g., AvailabilityOrchestrator, HealthMonitor, MaintenanceScheduler) if further decomposition is needed.

fr_dp_gate.reason: DP-D (condition-based monitoring + hierarchical availability management) directly addresses FR-NGHE-004 (achieve ≥0.97 availability). The DP is logically sound: predictive maintenance reduces unplanned downtime, and hierarchical FDIR minimizes recovery time. No circular logic or missing mappings detected. DP-D is implementable and testable.

diffusion_risks: Telemetry interface must be carefully designed to avoid overloading NGHE Solution with low-value data. Recommend tiered telemetry schema and adaptive sampling.
SafetyAlert interface must have clear semantics to distinguish safety-critical shutdowns from fault-driven shutdowns. Recommend explicit classification in SafetyAlert payload.
Tasking interface must support both maintenance directives and fault recovery commands without ambiguity. Recommend separate command types or explicit intent field.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: NGHE Solution is the clear owner of DP-D. No diffusion across Vehicle, Teleoperation, or Safety Services. All interfaces are system-level and already defined. Containment score 0.95 is high confidence.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: Verification of FR-NGHE-004 is feasible via continuous telemetry logging and statistical analysis. Recommend: (1) Define measurement period (e.g., rolling 30-day window); (2) Define uptime/downtime classification rules; (3) Implement automated availability dashboard; (4) Conduct field trials to validate measurement methodology.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: Allocation of FR-NGHE-004 to NGHE Solution is clear. Sub-FRs (FR-NGHE-004.1 through 004.4) are allocated to Vehicle, Teleoperation, Safety Services, and NGHE Solution respectively. Each sub-FR has a clear owner and measurable target.

recommended_revisions: Refine Telemetry interface specification to include health/condition data schema, transmission frequency, and latency SLA.
Refine SafetyAlert interface specification to include classification scheme (safety-critical vs. fault-driven) and impact on availability accounting.
Refine Tasking interface specification to distinguish maintenance directives from fault recovery commands.
Define availability calculation formula explicitly: Availability = Uptime / (Uptime + Downtime), where Downtime excludes planned maintenance and safety-critical shutdowns (if customer-approved).
Specify measurement period and evaluation window (e.g., rolling 30-day, monthly, quarterly).

decision.justification: FR-NGHE-004 is a system-level KPI that is not atomic. It depends on subsystem-level reliability (Vehicle MTBF), network robustness (Teleoperation link availability), and safety responsiveness (Safety Services detection latency). Decomposition into sub-FRs (FR-NGHE-004.1 through 004.4) is necessary to allocate responsibility and enable verification at subsystem level. DP-D and IM assignment (NGHE Solution) are sound and ready for next-level decomposition.
next_level_sub_frs: FR-NGHE-004.1: The Vehicle subsystem shall maintain mechanical and electrical readiness with mean time between failures (MTBF) ≥ X hours, verified via field reliability data and predictive maintenance alerts.
FR-NGHE-004.2: The Teleoperation subsystem shall maintain network link availability ≥ 0.99 and command-response latency ≤ Y ms, verified via continuous network monitoring and latency telemetry.

FR-NGHE-004.3: The Safety Services subsystem shall detect and respond to hazardous conditions within Z seconds without introducing spurious shutdowns, verified via safety test scenarios and false-positive rate analysis.

FR-NGHE-004.4: The NGHE Solution shall provide real-time availability monitoring, predictive maintenance alerts, and automated fault recovery coordination, verified via availability dashboard, alert accuracy, and recovery time metrics.

assumptions: Telemetry infrastructure can reliably transmit health data from all subsystems with sufficient frequency and fidelity.
Safety Services can distinguish between safety-critical shutdowns and fault-driven shutdowns.
Maintenance windows can be scheduled without excessive disruption to customer operations.
Customer-defined availability target is ≥0.97 (97%) unless otherwise specified.
Measurement period and uptime/downtime classification rules will be defined in detailed design.
open_risks: Telemetry overload: If all subsystems stream high-frequency health data, NGHE Solution may become a bottleneck. Mitigation: Implement tiered telemetry and adaptive sampling.
SafetyAlert ambiguity: If SafetyAlert semantics are unclear, availability accounting may be incorrect. Mitigation: Define explicit classification scheme.
Maintenance scheduling conflicts: If maintenance is too frequent or unpredictable, perceived availability may suffer. Mitigation: Expose scheduling constraints as configuration parameters.

Subsystem MTBF targets: If Vehicle, Teleoperation, or Safety Services cannot meet their allocated MTBF/availability targets, system-level availability will fall short. Mitigation: Conduct feasibility studies and adjust targets if needed.

Measurement methodology: If uptime/downtime classification is ambiguous or inconsistent, availability KPI may not be credible. Mitigation: Define measurement rules explicitly and validate via field trials.
evidence_needed: Telemetry data schema and transmission SLA specification.
SafetyAlert classification scheme and impact on availability accounting.
Tasking interface specification for maintenance and fault recovery commands.
Availability calculation formula and measurement period definition.
Field trial data validating availability measurement methodology.
Predictive maintenance model validation (accuracy, false-positive rate).
Fault recovery time analysis (mean time to recovery, MTTR).
